chunk_summary: Guidelines for interpreting test results and referral triggers in moderate acute malnutrition (MAM) non-response cases, covering anemia, UTI, celiac disease, and TB, with emphasis on diagnostic thresholds and appropriate follow-up actions.
learning_objectives: Understand referral triggers for anemia, UTI, celiac disease, and TB in MAM non-response cases.
Learn diagnostic thresholds for common investigations, including hemoglobin levels and urine culture results.
Recognize the importance of proper sampling techniques and follow-up in managing recurrent infections.
Identify when to escalate care for suspected TB or celiac disease based on clinical and diagnostic findings.
key_facts: Severe anemia is defined as hemoglobin <7 g/dL for ages 6–59 months (WHO).
For UTI, culture thresholds vary: suprapubic aspiration (any growth), catheter (>10^4 CFU/mL), clean-catch midstream (>10^5 CFU/mL).
Celiac disease diagnosis requires compatible serology and histology; mild intraepithelial lymphocytosis alone is not specific.
TB suspicion in MAM: wasting plus cough/fever ≥2 weeks or known contact requires immediate linkage to NTEP diagnostic pathways.
Proper urine sampling in non-toilet-trained children should use catheterization or suprapubic aspiration to avoid contamination.
reference_quote: Severe anemia by WHO classification (hemoglobin under 7 g/dL for ages 6–59 months) or any anemia with cardiorespiratory symptoms should prompt facility evaluation for cause and need for transfusion rather than outpatient iron alone.
document_key_topics: Urinary tract infection diagnosis and management
Tuberculosis suspicion and referral in malnourished children
